question_text: Cuscuta (dodder) is a well-known parasitic weed that causes severe losses in field crops like lucerne and niger — which specific type of parasitic plant is Cuscuta classified as based on its relationship with the host?

options.text: Total root parasite without chlorophyll
Partial stem parasite with chlorophyll
Total stem parasite without chlorophyll
Partial root parasite with chlorophyll

explanation: Cuscuta is a total stem parasite — it lacks chlorophyll and functional leaves, so it cannot photosynthesise and depends entirely on its host for water, nutrients, and carbohydrates. Its yellow-orange thread-like stems twine around host shoots and form haustoria that penetrate host tissues. Orobanche is a total root parasite; Loranthus is a partial stem parasite with chlorophyll; Striga is a partial root parasite.

elimination_hints.hint: Cuscuta is always seen attacking stems and shoots — never at roots.
Its characteristic yellow-orange colour signals absence of chlorophyll — hence total parasite.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Orobanche (broomrape) is a destructive root parasite of solanaceous and crucifer crops like tomato, brinjal, and mustard — which characteristic of Orobanche seeds makes it extremely difficult to control through conventional means?

options.text: Seeds are large, heavy, and remain on soil surface for easy removal
Seeds are dust-like, long-lived in soil, and germinate only on chemical signals from host roots
Seeds require freezing temperatures for germination not found in Indian conditions
Seeds germinate on any substrate including bare soil without host presence

explanation: Orobanche seeds are extraordinarily tiny (dust-like), remain viable in soil for 10–20 years (persistent seed bank), and undergo conditioned germination — they germinate only when they detect specific chemical signals (strigolactones) exuded by host roots. This host-dependent germination makes preventive measures difficult, as seeds lie dormant until a susceptible crop is planted. Trap crops that stimulate germination without supporting parasite establishment are a management strategy.

elimination_hints.hint: A is the opposite of reality — Orobanche seeds are microscopic dust.
C and D contradict the well-known strigolactone-dependent germination mechanism.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Water hyacinth (Eichhornia crassipes) is considered one of the world's worst aquatic weeds because of its explosive multiplication in water bodies — which biological control agent has been successfully used against water hyacinth in India?

options.text: Zygogramma bicolorata (Mexican beetle)
Neochetina eichhorniae (hyacinth weevil)
Cactoblastis cactorum (cactus moth)
Trichogramma chilonis (egg parasitoid)

explanation: Neochetina eichhorniae (and N. bruchi) are hyacinth weevils introduced as classical biocontrol agents against water hyacinth. Adult weevils feed on leaves while larvae bore into petioles and crowns, weakening and killing plants. Zygogramma targets Parthenium; Cactoblastis historically controlled prickly pear cactus in Australia; Trichogramma is an egg parasitoid of lepidopteran crop pests, not a weed biocontrol agent.

elimination_hints.hint: The name 'eichhorniae' literally comes from Eichhornia — direct taxonomic clue.
Zygogramma is famously used for Parthenium, not water hyacinth.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: The weed seed bank in soil plays a crucial role in determining weed emergence patterns season after season — which of the following factors most significantly prolongs weed seed longevity in the soil seed bank?

options.text: Shallow placement near soil surface exposing seeds to temperature fluctuations
Deep burial in soil combined with seed coat dormancy mechanisms
Continuous soil moisture maintained at field capacity throughout the year
High soil organic matter and biological activity in the topsoil layer

explanation: Deep burial protects weed seeds from predation, pathogen attack, and germination-triggering cues (light, temperature fluctuations). Combined with innate dormancy mechanisms (hard seed coat, chemical inhibitors, embryo dormancy), deep-buried seeds can remain viable for decades. Shallow seeds germinate or die quickly; consistent moisture promotes germination or decay; high biological activity accelerates seed decomposition. This is why zero-tillage can reduce weed seed bank depletion over time.

elimination_hints.hint: A promotes germination (light/temperature cues) — reducing longevity.
C and D both accelerate seed decay or germination — shortening life.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Systemic fungicides are preferred over contact fungicides in managing many plant diseases because of their distinct mode of action — what is the defining characteristic of a systemic fungicide like carbendazim or metalaxyl?

options.text: It forms a protective film on plant surface and kills pathogens on contact only
It is absorbed by plant tissues and translocated through xylem/phloem to protect new growth
It acts exclusively through fumigation in enclosed storage conditions
It requires high temperature and humidity for activation against pathogens

explanation: Systemic fungicides like carbendazim (benzimidazole) and metalaxyl (phenylamide) are absorbed by plant tissues and translocated through the vascular system, protecting both treated and newly emerging parts from pathogens. Contact fungicides like mancozeb and copper oxychloride form a surface barrier and require complete coverage. Systemic fungicides also offer curative action against established infections, not just prevention.

elimination_hints.hint: A describes contact fungicides (mancozeb, copper) — opposite of systemic action.
C and D describe fumigants and weather-dependent products, not systemic fungicides.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Wheat rust diseases, caused by Puccinia species, are among the most economically important cereal diseases worldwide — which of the three wheat rusts is considered the most destructive in the Indian subcontinent causing heavy yield losses?

options.text: Black rust / stem rust (Puccinia graminis)
Brown rust / leaf rust (Puccinia triticina)
Yellow rust / stripe rust (Puccinia striiformis)
All three are equally destructive in all Indian wheat zones

explanation: Black rust (stem rust) caused by Puccinia graminis f. sp. tritici is historically the most destructive wheat rust in India, capable of causing 70–100% yield losses under favourable conditions. It attacks stems, leaf sheaths, and glumes, breaking stems and causing lodging. Brown rust is common but less destructive; yellow rust is important in cooler northern hills. The Ug99 race remains a global threat though not yet established in India.

elimination_hints.hint: Black rust's historical devastation is well documented in Indian agricultural history.
Yellow rust is restricted to cooler hill regions — not widespread.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: The 'disease triangle' concept in plant pathology states that a plant disease can develop only when three essential components interact simultaneously — which of the following correctly lists all three components of the disease triangle?

options.text: Susceptible host, virulent pathogen, and favourable environment
Fertiliser application, seed treatment, and irrigation scheduling
Soil type, crop variety, and market price fluctuation
Insect vectors, wind dispersal, and human handling only

explanation: The disease triangle, a foundational concept in plant pathology, requires the simultaneous presence and interaction of three components: (1) a susceptible host plant, (2) a virulent pathogen, and (3) a favourable environment (temperature, humidity, moisture). If any one component is absent or unfavourable, disease does not develop. This principle underlies all disease management strategies — breaking any side of the triangle prevents disease.

elimination_hints.hint: B, C, and D mix agronomic and unrelated factors — not plant pathology fundamentals.
The disease triangle is a universal concept — its three sides are host, pathogen, and environment.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Pheromone traps are widely used in IPM programmes for monitoring and mass trapping of insect pests like Helicoverpa armigera and Spodoptera litura — what type of chemical signal do these traps primarily use to attract insects?

options.text: Aggregation pheromones produced by both male and female insects
Sex pheromones released by females to attract males of the same species
Alarm pheromones released when insects are disturbed or threatened
Trail pheromones used by ants and termites to mark food paths

explanation: Pheromone traps for lepidopteran pests like Helicoverpa, Spodoptera, and Plutella use synthetic analogues of female sex pheromones to attract male moths. By capturing males, these traps serve dual purposes — monitoring population dynamics (ETL decision-making) and mass trapping to reduce mating success. Species-specificity makes them non-harmful to beneficial insects. Aggregation and alarm pheromones are less commonly used in routine pest management.

elimination_hints.hint: D refers to ants/termites — not the target pests of agricultural pheromone traps.
Sex pheromones are the most common commercial formulation — standard IPM tool.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Pesticide labels in India are colour-coded with a coloured triangle at the bottom to indicate toxicity levels as per the Insecticides Rules 1971 — which colour indicates an EXTREMELY TOXIC (Class Ia) pesticide formulation?

options.text: Bright red (extremely toxic / danger)
Bright yellow (highly toxic / poison)
Bright blue (moderately toxic / caution)
Bright green (slightly toxic / low hazard)

explanation: India's colour code for pesticide toxicity (as per Insecticides Rules 1971) uses red for extremely toxic (Class Ia — oral LD50 < 50 mg/kg), yellow for highly toxic (Class Ib), blue for moderately toxic (Class II), and green for slightly toxic (Class III/U). The triangular colour band on the bottom of pesticide labels is a mandatory safety indicator, allowing even illiterate users to identify hazard levels visually.

elimination_hints.hint: Red universally signals highest danger — matches the extremely toxic class.
Green signals safety/low hazard in most pesticide systems globally.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI) is a crucial pesticide safety parameter that must be strictly followed by farmers before harvesting treated crops — what does PHI specifically represent in pesticide regulation?

options.text: The minimum time between two consecutive pesticide applications
The minimum time between last pesticide application and crop harvest to ensure residues are below MRL
The total number of pesticide sprays allowed per crop season
The maximum dose of pesticide per hectare permitted by regulation

explanation: Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI) is the minimum number of days that must elapse between the last pesticide application and harvest, ensuring that pesticide residues in the produce decline to levels at or below the Maximum Residue Limit (MRL). Adhering to PHI is essential for food safety and export compliance. It is NOT the interval between sprays (that's the spray interval) or the total number of applications (application frequency).

elimination_hints.hint: The word 'pre-harvest' clearly relates to the period BEFORE harvest.
C and D describe application frequency and dose — unrelated to the pre-harvest concept.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each major plant disease with its correct causal organism and the crop it affects most severely under Indian agricultural conditions.
column_b: Ustilago tritici affecting wheat — seeds replaced by black spore masses in place of grain
Magnaporthe oryzae affecting rice — diamond-shaped spindle lesions on leaves with grey centres
Phytophthora infestans affecting potato — historically caused the Irish Potato Famine of 1845
Colletotrichum falcatum affecting sugarcane — internal reddening of stem with cross-wise white patches

correct_mapping.Late blight: Phytophthora infestans affecting potato — historically caused the Irish Potato Famine of 1845
correct_mapping.Red rot: Colletotrichum falcatum affecting sugarcane — internal reddening of stem with cross-wise white patches
correct_mapping.Rice blast: Magnaporthe oryzae affecting rice — diamond-shaped spindle lesions on leaves with grey centres
correct_mapping.Loose smut: Ustilago tritici affecting wheat — seeds replaced by black spore masses in place of grain

options.text: Late blight→Phytophthora/potato; Red rot→Colletotrichum/sugarcane; Rice blast→Magnaporthe/rice; Loose smut→Ustilago/wheat
Late blight→Ustilago/wheat; Red rot→Phytophthora/potato; Rice blast→Colletotrichum/sugarcane; Loose smut→Magnaporthe/rice
Late blight→Magnaporthe/rice; Red rot→Ustilago/wheat; Rice blast→Phytophthora/potato; Loose smut→Colletotrichum/sugarcane
Late blight→Colletotrichum/sugarcane; Red rot→Magnaporthe/rice; Rice blast→Ustilago/wheat; Loose smut→Phytophthora/potato

explanation: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans, an oomycete) caused the 1845 Irish Famine and remains the most important potato disease. Red rot (Colletotrichum falcatum) is a major sugarcane disease with characteristic internal reddening interrupted by white patches. Rice blast (Magnaporthe oryzae) produces spindle-shaped grey lesions. Loose smut (Ustilago tritici) replaces wheat grain with black spore masses, transmitted through infected seed.

elimination_hints.hint: 'Late blight' and 'Irish Famine' are historically inseparable — direct match.
Smut diseases are all caused by Ustilago species in cereals.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each herbicide with its correct mode of action at the biochemical level which determines its effectiveness against target weed species in agricultural fields.
column_b: Synthetic auxin causing uncontrolled growth and epinasty in broadleaf weeds
Photosystem II (PSII) inhibitor blocking electron transport in chloroplasts
Microtubule inhibitor disrupting cell division in emerging weed seedlings
EPSPS enzyme inhibitor blocking shikimate pathway for aromatic amino acids

correct_mapping.Glyphosate: EPSPS enzyme inhibitor blocking shikimate pathway for aromatic amino acids
correct_mapping.Atrazine: Photosystem II (PSII) inhibitor blocking electron transport in chloroplasts
correct_mapping.2,4-D: Synthetic auxin causing uncontrolled growth and epinasty in broadleaf weeds
correct_mapping.Pendimethalin: Microtubule inhibitor disrupting cell division in emerging weed seedlings

options.text: Glyphosate→EPSPS inhibitor; Atrazine→PSII inhibitor; 2,4-D→Synthetic auxin; Pendimethalin→Microtubule inhibitor
Glyphosate→PSII inhibitor; Atrazine→Synthetic auxin; 2,4-D→Microtubule inhibitor; Pendimethalin→EPSPS inhibitor
Glyphosate→Synthetic auxin; Atrazine→Microtubule inhibitor; 2,4-D→EPSPS inhibitor; Pendimethalin→PSII inhibitor
Glyphosate→Microtubule inhibitor; Atrazine→EPSPS inhibitor; 2,4-D→PSII inhibitor; Pendimethalin→Synthetic auxin

explanation: Each herbicide has a distinct biochemical target. Glyphosate inhibits EPSPS (5-enolpyruvylshikimate-3-phosphate synthase), blocking synthesis of aromatic amino acids. Atrazine binds to the D1 protein of PSII, halting photosynthetic electron transport. 2,4-D mimics the plant auxin IAA, causing abnormal uncontrolled growth in dicots. Pendimethalin disrupts microtubule formation, preventing mitotic cell division in germinating seedlings.

elimination_hints.hint: EPSPS is unique to glyphosate — a distinctive clue used in many exams.
Atrazine's PSII blocking is why treated weeds turn yellow (chlorosis) and die.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each biocontrol agent used in Indian agriculture with its correct target pest or disease that it effectively manages in crop ecosystems.
column_b: Egg parasitoid of lepidopteran pests like sugarcane borers and cotton bollworm
Entomopathogenic fungus causing white muscardine disease in wide range of insect pests
Antagonistic fungus against soil-borne diseases like root rot, wilt, and damping-off
Plant growth-promoting bacterium with antifungal activity against soil-borne pathogens

correct_mapping.Trichoderma viride: Antagonistic fungus against soil-borne diseases like root rot, wilt, and damping-off
correct_mapping.Pseudomonas fluorescens: Plant growth-promoting bacterium with antifungal activity against soil-borne pathogens
correct_mapping.Beauveria bassiana: Entomopathogenic fungus causing white muscardine disease in wide range of insect pests
correct_mapping.Trichogramma chilonis: Egg parasitoid of lepidopteran pests like sugarcane borers and cotton bollworm

options.text: Trichoderma→Antagonistic fungus; Pseudomonas→PGPR antifungal; Beauveria→Entomopathogen; Trichogramma→Egg parasitoid
Trichoderma→Egg parasitoid; Pseudomonas→Entomopathogen; Beauveria→PGPR antifungal; Trichogramma→Antagonistic fungus
Trichoderma→Entomopathogen; Pseudomonas→Antagonistic fungus; Beauveria→Egg parasitoid; Trichogramma→PGPR antifungal
Trichoderma→PGPR antifungal; Pseudomonas→Egg parasitoid; Beauveria→Antagonistic fungus; Trichogramma→Entomopathogen

explanation: Trichoderma viride is a widely used fungal antagonist for soil-borne diseases (applied via seed treatment/soil drenching). Pseudomonas fluorescens is a PGPR with both growth promotion and disease suppression effects. Beauveria bassiana is an entomopathogenic fungus causing white muscardine in many insects. Trichogramma chilonis is a tiny wasp that parasitises moth eggs — extensively used against sugarcane borers, cotton bollworm, and maize stalk borer.

elimination_hints.hint: Trichogramma wasps attack eggs only — the name is often linked with 'egg parasitoid'.
Beauveria's 'white muscardine' symptom is a signature fungal infection.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Analyse the Assertion and Reason below concerning strigolactone signals and parasitic weed germination, then choose the option that best describes their relationship.
assertion: Breeding crop varieties that produce low levels of strigolactones in root exudates is a promising strategy to manage Orobanche and Striga infestations in host crops.
reason: Strigolactones released by host roots serve as essential germination stimulants for parasitic weed seeds, and reducing their production starves the parasite of its germination cue.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect

explanation: The assertion is correct — breeding for low strigolactone production is an active research area for Striga-resistant sorghum and pearl millet varieties. The reason correctly explains the mechanism: strigolactones are the chemical cues that trigger germination of Orobanche and Striga seeds. Without these signals, parasite seeds remain dormant in the soil. The reason directly justifies why the strategy works.

elimination_hints.hint: The strigolactone-based germination mechanism is a well-documented discovery.
Low-strigolactone varieties (e.g., Striga-resistant sorghum lines) exist and work in practice.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Evaluate the following Assertion and Reason about endosulfan usage in India and select the option that correctly describes their relationship.
assertion: The Government of India has imposed a complete ban on the manufacture, sale, and use of endosulfan pesticide across the country.
reason: Endosulfan is a persistent organic pollutant that bioaccumulates in food chains and has been linked to severe health effects including birth defects in exposed human populations of Kerala.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect

explanation: The assertion is correct — India imposed a nationwide ban on endosulfan in 2011 following a Supreme Court order. The reason correctly explains the basis: endosulfan is listed under the Stockholm Convention as a persistent organic pollutant; it bioaccumulates in fat tissues, and severe health consequences (including congenital defects and neurological disorders) were documented in Kasaragod district of Kerala following aerial spraying in cashew plantations. The reason directly justifies the ban.

elimination_hints.hint: The Kasaragod/Kerala endosulfan tragedy is a well-known public health case.
Stockholm Convention listing and India's 2011 ban are documented facts.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Read the following Assertion and Reason about herbicide rotation practice and determine whether both are correct and if the reason explains the assertion accurately.
assertion: Rotating herbicides with different modes of action across seasons is strongly recommended to delay the development of herbicide-resistant weed biotypes in agricultural fields.
reason: Weeds resistant to one mode of action are typically susceptible to a different mode of action, so alternating targets prevents selection of multiple-resistance biotypes.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect

explanation: The assertion is correct — herbicide rotation is a fundamental principle of resistance management endorsed globally. The reason correctly explains the logic: target-site resistance is usually specific to one mode of action, so biotypes resistant to (say) ALS inhibitors remain susceptible to PSII inhibitors. Alternating modes prevents continuous selection pressure on any single target, slowing resistance build-up. This is the same logic used in antibiotic rotation.

elimination_hints.hint: Herbicide rotation is standard IPM/IWM advice — assertion is textbook.
The biochemical specificity of resistance mechanisms supports the reason.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Arrange the following steps of seed treatment with a fungicide + biocontrol agent combination in the correct sequence as per recommended seed treatment protocols for field crops.
items: Clean and weigh the seed required for the area to be sown
Apply chemical fungicide like carbendazim or thiram at recommended dose per kg of seed
Apply biocontrol agent Trichoderma viride after the chemical has dried on seed surface
Dry the treated seed in shade and sow within recommended time window

options.text: Clean and weigh → Apply chemical fungicide → Apply Trichoderma → Dry and sow
Apply Trichoderma → Clean and weigh → Dry and sow → Apply chemical fungicide
Apply chemical fungicide → Apply Trichoderma → Clean and weigh → Dry and sow
Dry and sow → Apply Trichoderma → Apply chemical fungicide → Clean and weigh

explanation: Correct seed treatment sequence: (1) clean and weigh seed to determine dose of chemicals; (2) apply fungicide first — never after Trichoderma since chemicals would kill the biocontrol agent; (3) add Trichoderma only after the fungicide layer dries; (4) dry in shade and sow promptly. This order is critical — if biocontrol is added first, the chemical fungicide applied over it will eliminate the beneficial organism.

elimination_hints.hint: Cleaning and weighing must come first — you need to know the dose.
Chemical before biocontrol is a universal rule to preserve biocontrol viability.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Arrange the following steps in the correct sequence for conducting safe spraying of a chemical pesticide in a crop field following good agricultural practices and safety norms.
items: Read the pesticide label carefully for dose, safety, and PHI information
Wear personal protective equipment including gloves, mask, goggles, and boots
Calibrate sprayer, prepare spray solution using correct dose, and spray during cool hours avoiding wind
After spraying, wash body and equipment thoroughly and dispose of empty containers safely

options.text: Read label → Wear PPE → Calibrate and spray → Wash and dispose
Wear PPE → Read label → Wash and dispose → Calibrate and spray
Calibrate and spray → Wear PPE → Read label → Wash and dispose
Wash and dispose → Calibrate and spray → Wear PPE → Read label

explanation: Safe spraying sequence: always begin by reading the label for correct dose, toxicity class (colour code), and PHI. Then don PPE before handling concentrates. Next, calibrate the sprayer and mix solution using appropriate dose; spray during early morning or evening to avoid heat, sun, and wind drift. Finally, wash body thoroughly, clean equipment, and dispose of empty containers safely (triple-rinse, puncture, bury — never reuse).

elimination_hints.hint: Reading the label is the universal first step before any chemical handling.
Washing/disposal is always the final cleanup step after any spraying operation.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Evaluate whether the following statement is true or false: Parthenium hysterophorus (congress grass / carrot weed) is a noxious invasive weed in India that causes allergic contact dermatitis in humans and reduces milk yield in cattle that accidentally consume it.

options.text: True
False

explanation: This statement is true. Parthenium hysterophorus, native to tropical America and accidentally introduced to India in 1955, is now a widespread invasive weed. It causes severe allergic contact dermatitis and respiratory allergies in humans through contact with its pollen and trichomes. In cattle, accidental consumption causes bitter milk, reduced yield, and in extreme cases death. Zygogramma bicolorata is the biocontrol agent used against this weed.

elimination_hints.hint: Parthenium's health hazards to humans and livestock are extensively documented.
The introduction of Zygogramma beetle specifically targets Parthenium — itself evidence of the problem.

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Study the diagram showing pesticide toxicity colour codes as per the Insecticides Rules 1971 of India, then identify the correct matching of colour, hazard class, and a representative pesticide example.

options.text: Red triangle — extremely toxic (Class Ia) — example: phorate, methyl parathion
Yellow triangle — slightly toxic (Class III) — example: glyphosate, atrazine
Blue triangle — highly toxic (Class Ib) — example: endosulfan, monocrotophos
Green triangle — extremely toxic (Class Ia) — example: carbendazim, mancozeb

explanation: From the diagram, the red triangle denotes extremely toxic (Class Ia, LD50 < 50 mg/kg) pesticides such as phorate and methyl parathion. Yellow is highly toxic (Class Ib, not slightly toxic). Blue is moderately toxic (Class II), not highly toxic. Green indicates slightly toxic (Class III/U) — not extremely toxic. The colour-class-example combinations in options B, C, and D are all mismatched.

elimination_hints.hint: Red is universally the danger colour — matches extremely toxic (Class Ia).
Green always signals safety/low hazard — never extremely toxic (Class Ia).

topic: Weed Control and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
